# Open Challenges in AI-Assisted Software Development

Despite rapid convergence on practices like harness engineering, upfront planning, and agent orchestration, several fundamental problems remain unsolved as software development shifts toward agent-driven workflows. These challenges are not minor friction points but structural obstacles that will shape how far and how fast the transformation can go.

### Code maintainability and entropy

Agent-generated code accumulates technical debt in ways that differ from human-written code. Output may be functionally correct — passing tests, meeting specifications — while being poorly structured, inconsistent in style, or resistant to future modification. The problem is subtle because it often passes review at the individual pull request level but degrades the codebase cumulatively. Some teams have begun deploying dedicated "garbage collection" agents that scan for architectural violations and inconsistencies, but this remains an emerging and incomplete practice. The deeper question is whether agent-generated codebases require fundamentally different maintainability strategies than human-written ones, or whether existing software engineering principles simply need more rigorous mechanical enforcement.

### Verification at scale

Agents routinely mark work as complete without adequate end-to-end verification. Without explicit prompting, they fail to recognize when something does not actually work — a tendency toward premature declaration of success that compounds dangerously when agents operate autonomously or in parallel. Even when verification tooling exists, the tools themselves introduce blind spots. Browser automation frameworks, for instance, may not expose browser-native UI elements like alert modals, causing agents to miss bugs that a human tester would catch immediately. Each tool in the verification chain has its own jagged frontier of capability, and agents lack the judgment to recognize when they are operating near the edge of what their tools can see. Verification remains the weakest link in the autonomous agent workflow, and no current approach fully closes the gap between "tests pass" and "the feature actually works."

### The brownfield problem

Nearly all prominent success stories involve greenfield projects or purpose-built harnesses constructed from scratch. Applying these techniques to a mature codebase — one with a decade of accumulated architectural decisions, inconsistent testing practices, patchy documentation, and implicit conventions that exist only in the heads of long-tenured engineers — is a qualitatively harder problem. The analogy is apt: enabling a static analysis tool on a codebase that has never had one produces a flood of alerts that overwhelms rather than helps. A legacy codebase lacks the clean architectural boundaries, comprehensive test suites, and machine-readable documentation that harness engineering depends on. Building those foundations retroactively while maintaining a production system is expensive, slow, and politically difficult. How agent-driven development translates to brownfield environments remains largely uncharted territory.

### Cultural adoption and the meta-work burden

None of these transformations happen by default. Someone at each organization must do the deliberate, often unglamorous work of figuring out how agents integrate into existing workflows — creating harnesses, defining processes, writing documentation, and iterating based on results. Practitioners report that a significant portion of their time goes to this meta-work rather than to the product itself. The investment compounds — every improvement to documentation, tooling, or architecture pays dividends across all future agent sessions — but the upfront cost is substantial and the returns are initially invisible.

The cultural dimension may be the hardest challenge of all. Engineers whose professional identity and satisfaction are rooted in the craft of writing code often resist a shift toward planning, reviewing, and orchestrating. Those oriented toward shipping products tend to adapt more readily. Organizations must navigate this unevenly distributed willingness to change while simultaneously investing in harness infrastructure whose payoff is deferred and difficult to measure. The combination of high upfront cost, uncertain near-term returns, and identity-level resistance to role change makes cultural adoption a barrier that no amount of tooling improvement can fully resolve on its own.

## See Also

- [[z20260315-008|The Transformation of the Software Engineering Role in the Age of AI Coding Agents]] — grounds the cultural and role-identity challenges discussed here in the broader shift from implementation to orchestration and review
- [[z20260315-001|Agent Harness Architecture]] — elaborates on the harness infrastructure whose absence in brownfield environments and whose construction cost in any environment underlie several of the open challenges identified here
- [[z20260315-003|Long-Horizon Autonomous Agent Execution]] — exemplifies the verification and coherence problems that become acute at extended time horizons, illustrating why premature completion and inadequate self-checking remain unsolved
- [[z20260315-004|Co-Evolution of Model Training and Harness Design]] — contrasts the optimistic trajectory of model-harness co-improvement with the structural barriers to adoption and verification described here
